props.covers: The pure pointer-interaction state machine, alignment/distribution snapping, in-memory clipboard, and edge-pan auto-scroll that sit between raw pointer events and the model-layer reducer.

text.insert: This layer sits between raw pointer events and the model-layer reducer. A thin DOM adapter in
converts native pointer events into normalized, world-space

s; everything downstream of that — the pointer state machine, snapping math, in-memory clipboard, and edge-pan auto-scroll — is plain, DOM-free logic that consumes normalized events and document/selection state and emits typed
s for the model-layer reducer to apply.
The core of this layer is
— a pure pointer-interaction state machine modeled on term-draw's draw-state.ts architecture. Its entry point,
, a normalized pointer event, and a read-only
(document, selection, tool, viewport, and an optional snap resolver hook) and returns the next state plus any
s to dispatch and an ephemeral

to render that frame (marquee rect, snap/distribution guides, spacing hints, drop target, connector-drag preview, inline label-edit request, place-tool ghost). No DOM access happens anywhere in this module — it is testable with plain objects.
union has seven kinds, each with its own step function dispatched from

. A pointer-down here branches on hit-test kind into a resize, connector-endpoint-drag, connector-create, place, pressing, or (for a double-click) an immediate inline-label-edit request — including the empty-canvas double-click case, which predicts the new text object's id with the same deterministic
's reducer uses, so the editor can open the label textarea before React has even run the reducer.
. A sub-threshold release resolves as a click (applying a deferred shift-toggle, or clearing selection on empty canvas); crossing the threshold promotes the gesture into
. This is also where a section drag picks up its members: promotion to move expands the dragged id set to the section's recorded parentId descendants (recursively, via
), folded into the same move gesture rather than a separate mechanism. Promotion is also lock-gated: before entering move, every id in the dragged set is checked with
— if any dragged object is itself a locked section, or sits under a parentId-ancestor section with
, the gesture returns to idle instead of promoting.
) — dragging one or more selected objects, handled by
. Each move computes live snap correction (via
if the host supplies one, else
), applies it uniformly across the dragged set, tracks a live drop-target section — probed at the center of the primary dragged object's current bounds — for release-time
(assigning membership on drop into a section, clearing it on open canvas), and dispatches
captured before any snap correction ran, so a snap can never leak into cancelled-drag geometry.
deferral (small targets, no click semantics) and commit to the gesture immediately; the anchored opposite edge/corner never moves, and both dimensions clamp to
(48 world units). Only the edges the active handle actually moves are snap-corrected. The handle-down entry path is gated by the same
check — a locked section (either mode), or any object under a locked-
section, refuses resize and the pointer-down falls back to idle.
) — rubber-band selection over empty canvas with the select tool, handled by
. Tracks a normalized bounds rect as the pointer moves; on release, every object intersecting the bounds becomes the selection (or is unioned into it, for a shift-additive marquee).

. Starts when a creatable tool (rectangle, process, decision, text, sticky, source-node, annotation-marker, document, person, database, chat, section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon) is armed and the pointer goes down. A sub-threshold release creates a default-size object centered at the point; a drag creates an object sized to the normalized,
-clamped dragged rect. Either way the tool reverts to
and the new object becomes the selection, adopting a parentId when placed inside a section.
) — reconnecting an existing connection's endpoint, or dragging a brand-new connector from an object's edge port. Both resolve the live connect-target every move via
, which calls the ported AFFiNE connection cascade —
— to pick, in priority order, an anchor snap (within 8 view px), a nearest-outline-point snap (within 8 world px), or a coarse inside-the-shape candidate. On release, an endpoint drag dispatches
when dropped on a candidate, or
(create-and-connect as one history entry) when dropped on empty canvas.
— an exported pure helper that answers "may this object be dragged or resized?": true when the object itself carries
(either mode), or when the walk up its
. The walk is cycle-safe — a visited set plus an iteration cap bounded by the document's object count — so a corrupt parent cycle degrades to "not locked" rather than hanging the pointer pipeline.
, coalesces a rapid stream of values (pointermove positions, wheel deltas) down to at most one commit per animation frame — every
overwrites the pending value and schedules a frame only if one isn't already pending,
commits synchronously on pointerup so a drag-end is never dropped behind a frame that never fires, and
discards without committing (used on unmount). It takes an injectable
so tests can supply a deterministic fake instead of a real rAF loop.
is the host-initiated cancel path used by Escape-key handling elsewhere in the editor: given the current
, it restores the pre-gesture geometry for an in-progress
) and returns to idle — the same restore path a pointercancel event triggers, just reachable without a corresponding pointer event.

props.title: interaction.ts is a pure state machine, decoupled from React/DOM

text.insert: takes only normalized pointer events and plain data (document, selection, tool, viewport) as input, and returns only data (next state, actions, overlay) as output — it never touches the DOM, React state, or refs directly. This buys two concrete things. First, gesture logic that would otherwise require a real browser and pointer-event simulation — most notably the connection cascade resolved by

during a connector drag, and section drop-targeting during a move — becomes unit-testable with plain objects and a fake clock/scheduler, which is exactly what the interaction test suite below exercises. Second, and more fundamentally, it means every

the editor ever dispatches can originate from either a live pointer gesture stepped through this machine, or from external automation (tests, scripted samples, an agent) constructing the same actions directly — both paths converge on the identical reducer the model layer exposes, so there is exactly one way a document ever actually changes. This mirrors the model layer's own single-reducer decision: interaction.ts is the pointer-driven producer of actions, but never a second mutation authority alongside it.
describes that reducer and its action vocabulary in full.
computes alignment guides for dragged/resized objects against other objects' edges and centers.
compares each of the moving bounds' three lines per axis (near edge, center, far edge) against the same three lines on up to

(100) nearest candidates — siblings sharing the dragged set's parent, plus every section regardless of nesting — within a caller-supplied screen-space threshold (6px, divided by zoom so the snap feels constant at any zoom level, per
in interaction.ts). The closest match per axis wins; if multiple candidates tie at that closest distance on the same guide line, their spans merge into one visual guide.

separately detects equal-gap runs of objects along an axis and surfaces a spacing hint chip when the moving object touches one end of an equal-gap group — a FigJam-style "these three are evenly spaced" affordance. Grid alignment is handled by
) only applies when the drag started grid-aligned; otherwise values round to clean integers so objects avoid sub-pixel drift, a PD5-era fallback rule.
composes point/edge alignment with the ported AFFiNE equal-spacing ("distribution") snap —

— using AFFiNE's own priority rule: on a given axis, point alignment wins if it produced a non-zero correction, and the distribution search only runs (and can win) on an axis where alignment found nothing. This is the function
's default path calls, and what any host-supplied
should wrap if it wants both alignment and distribution snapping composed identically.
implements an in-memory clipboard — a module-level
captures the current object selection plus every descendant (via
, so copying a section brings its children along), plus every connection whose both endpoints land inside the captured set, deep-cloned via
(the copied selection's bounds center) used later to compute the paste offset. The editor's hotkey handler (
) stores the result in the module singleton via
on Cmd/Ctrl-C. On paste, it reads the payload back with

, which re-clones the objects and connections and translates every geometry — and every explicit connection waypoint, since W4 waypoints are stored as absolute world coordinates — by the delta between a target drop point (or a default 24-unit offset when pasting without one) and the recorded
, so a fan of connectors pastes intact rather than re-routing from scratch. The resulting objects and connections are dispatched in a single
action, so the whole paste — however many objects and connections it contains — lands as one reducer call and one undo step.
auto-pans the viewport while a drag, marquee, or connector-endpoint gesture is in progress and the pointer sits inside a
-px region near a stage edge, so the user can drag past the visible viewport bounds without releasing the pointer. Its sole export,
, takes the pointer's stage-relative position, the stage's screen-space size, the edge band width, and a max speed, and returns a
delta to add to the viewport's pan each animation frame (matching
). Each axis is computed independently, so a pointer parked in a corner yields nonzero
simultaneously. Velocity ramps linearly from 0 at
px away from the edge up to
right at the edge (or beyond, if the pointer has left the stage bounds entirely); a pointer more than
px from every edge — the "safe" interior — or a non-positive
exercises this layer purely at the data level, with no browser:
covers the general gesture set (press/click/drag thresholds, move, resize, marquee, place);
targets the connector-endpoint-drag/connector-create candidate resolution against the AFFiNE cascade (anchor/outline/inside priority);
with a fake scheduler to verify collapse-to-latest, synchronous flush, and cancel-without-commit;
injection hook overrides the default snap computation during a move gesture without ever affecting cancelled-drag restoration;
cover alignment-guide and equal-spacing/distribution snap math respectively; and

's velocity ramp and corner/out-of-bounds cases. Because none of this layer touches the DOM, every one of these suites runs as fast, deterministic unit tests rather than browser-driven component tests.

Connector routing is closely related to this layer: connections get re-routed live during the move, resize, and connector-drag gestures described above, driven by geometry changes this state machine dispatches. See
for the connector routing and cascade-resolution behavior itself.
